Facts.text: Solar eclipse on this date might indicate the birth of the legendary Heracles in Thebes, Greece
Ottaviano de Montecello is elected Antipope Victor IV
Flemish pretender Perkin Warbeck is acclaimed as English King Richard IV on Bodmin Moor in Cornwall
Trial against heretic John Pistorius ends in The Hague
Dutch fleet bombards Banten, Java
Earl of Essex and Irish rebel Tyrone sign treaty
City of Boston, Massachusetts, is founded
Germany, England, and the Netherlands sign an anti-French covenant
Treaty of Baden is signed by the Holy Roman Empire and France, ending the War of the Spanish Succession; France retains Alsace and Landau, and Austria gains the east bank of the Rhine
Zion AME Church is dedicated in New York City
"Uncle Sam" is first used to refer to the US by Troy Post of New York
Carl III of Sweden-Norway is crowned King of Norway in Trondheim
A US Federal naval expedition arrives off Sabine Pass in the Gulf of Mexico, blockading the Texas coast
Bay of Biscay: British warship HMS Captain capsizes, 500 are killed
George Ligowsky patents a device to throw clay pigeons for trapshooters
Forces of British administrator Captain Frederick Lugard reach Kavalli, Equatoria
A. H. Whiting wins the first automobile race held on a closed-circuit track in Cranston, Rhode Island
George Davis (Giants) hits a home run off Sport McAllister (Spiders) in a doubleheader
The Peace of Peking puts an end to the Boxer Rebellion in China
In Australia, the entire nation observes a "day of humiliation" and prays for rain as a terrible drought kills livestock and threatens crops; rain begins to fall on September 10
Federation of American Motorcyclists is formed in New York
British forces in Tibet force the 13th Dalai Lama to sign a treaty granting Britain trading posts in Tibet and guaranteeing that Tibet will not concede territory to foreign powers
Adolph Sutro's ornate Cliff House in San Francisco is destroyed by fire
Ocean liner RMS Lusitania begins her maiden voyage sailing from Liverpool to New York City
Eugene Lefebvre becomes the first pilot to die in an aircraft while test piloting a new French-built Wright biplane at Juvisy
In The Hague, the International Court arbitrates a fishing rights dispute between the US and Newfoundland (still separate from Canada)
NY Post Office Building opens to the public
Johnny Gruelle patents his Raggedy Ann doll (US Patent D47789)
St. Louis Terriers' Dave Davenport no-hits the Chicago Whales (Federal League) 3-0
Giants beat Brooklyn 4-1 to launch New York's record 26-game winning streak
Workers' Compensation Act is passed by US Congress
Turkish forces recapture the Aegean city of Aydin from Greek occupation during the Greco-Turkish War
Boston Red Sox Howard Ehmke no-hits Philadelphia A's, 4-0
Interpol is formed in Vienna
Philo Farnsworth demonstrates the first use of his electronic television in San Francisco
University of Minas Gerais is founded in Belo Horizonte, Brazil, by Governor Antônio Carlos
Sophie Treadwell's "Machinal" premieres in New York City
Earl Grace ends the National League catcher record streak of 110 consecutive errorless games
Romantic comedy film "Page Miss Glory," starring Marion Davies, premieres in the US
The last surviving member of the marsupial thylacine species, Benjamin, dies alone in his cage at Hobart Zoo, Hobart, Tasmania, Australia
Radio New York Worldwide (World Radio University Listeners or WRUL) begins broadcasting
Treaty of Craiova: Romania loses Southern Dobrudja to Bulgaria
First flight of the Consolidated B-32 Dominator
Transport #29 departs with French Jews to Nazi Germany
Fire in decrepit old Gulf Hotel kills 45 people in Houston, Texas
Dutch Resistance fighter Jaap Musch arrested in Nijverdal, Netherlands
SS General Kurt Meyer takes Durnal, Belgium
Strongest hurricane of the century in the Netherlands (wind force 12)
Allied Victory Parade held in Berlin, Germany with representation from armed forces of the Soviet Union, the United States, the United Kingdom, and France
Joe Kuhel hits an inside-the-park home run, the only homer hit by a member of the Washington Senators team all season at their home field, Griffith Stadium
Battles between Hindus and Muslims in New Delhi, India
First use of synthetic rubber in asphaltic concrete in Akron, Ohio
Knockshinnoch disaster: A coal mine flood traps 129 miners in New Cumnock, Ayrshire, Scotland; all but thirteen are saved over the next two days
Monasteries shut down in Hungary
General Naguib forms the Egyptian government and becomes prime minister
NY Yankees Johnny Mize's pinch-hit grand slam gives Yanks a 5-1 win at Washington, giving him a home run in all 15 major league parks
Outfielder Don Grate of Tennessee throws a baseball a record 434 ft 1 in (132.3 m)
Roy Campanellasets a catcher record of 125 RBIs en route to 142 RBIs
Integration begins in Washington, D.C., and Baltimore, Maryland, public schools
New York YankeeWhitey Fordbecomes the fifth MLB pitcher to hurl consecutive one-hitters
Bell X-2 sets an unofficial manned aircraft altitude record of 126,000 feet (38,404.8 meters)
WWL TV Channel 4 in New Orleans, LA (CBS) begins broadcasting

American weightlifter Charles Vinci wins his second consecutive bantamweight gold medal at the Rome Olympics with a world-record-equaling three-lift (snatch, clean & jerk, overhead press) total of 345.0 kilograms
Danish yachtsman Paul Elvstrøm wins the Finn class at the Rome Olympics, his fourth consecutive Olympic gold medal in the category
Future Constantine II, the last King of Greece, wins the Dragon class sailing gold medal at the Rome Olympics
Japan wins the men's gymnastics team combined exercises gold for the first of five consecutive Olympics at the Rome Games, beating the Soviet Union by just 2.5 points
Lyudmila Shevtsova sets an Olympic record in the women's 800 m (2:04.3)
American Bandstand moves to California and airs once a week on a Saturday
Two-building Pro Football Hall of Fame in Canton, Ohio, officially opens
WCTI TV Channel 12 in New Bern, NC (ABC) begins broadcasting
China announces that it will reinforce its troops on the Indian border
Hurricane Betsy kills 74 in Florida, Mississippi, and Louisiana
KTNE TV Channel 13 in Alliance, NE (PBS) begins broadcasting
USSR conducts a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeastern Kazakhstan
Donald Boyles sets a record for the highest parachute jump from a bridge by leaping off the 956 ft (291 m) Royal Gorge Bridge in Colorado
Fighting between Arab guerrillas and government forces in Amman, Jordan
Jerry Lewis's 5th Muscular Dystrophy telethon
Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine hijacks four planes and forces them to land at Dawson's Field, Jordan
White Sox use a record 41 players in a doubleheader and lose both games

American athletes Vincent Matthews and Wayne Collett finish 1-2 in the 400 m at the Munich Olympics, act casually on the medal stand, and do not face the flag during the anthem, resulting in a lifetime ban from the Olympics
American Rod Milburn wins the 110 m hurdles gold medal at the Munich Olympics with a world record of 13.24, just 0.10 seconds ahead of Frenchman Guy Drut
East German sprinter Renate Stecher runs a world record-equaling 22.40 in the 200 m final to repeat her 100 m win over Australia's Raelene Boyle at the Munich Olympics
Future world professional cycling road race champion Hennie Kuiper of the Netherlands wins the road race gold medal at the Munich Olympics in 4:14:37
Nadezhda Chizhova of the Soviet Union throws a world record 21.03 m to win the women's shot put at the Munich Olympics
Mike Storen becomes the American Basketball Association's fourth commissioner
Cincinnati Reds win the earliest NL division title
Ethiopia severs diplomatic relations with Somalia
Watergate conspirator G. Gordon Liddy is released from prison after serving 4 years and 4.5 months
First game of the Boston Massacre: NY Yankees beat the Red Sox 15-3
Entertainment and Sports Programming Network (ESPN) launches; the first show is SportsCenter

32nd Emmy Awards, notable for proceeding despite 51 of the 52 nominated performers boycotting the event due to a strike by members of the Screen Actors Guild: "Taxi," "Lou Grant," Ed Asner, and Barbara Bel Geddes win
Cape Verde adopts its constitution
Earnest Gray becomes the second New York Giant to score four touchdowns (against St. Louis)
Oakland A's pitch a record 78th complete game of the season
Turkish coup d'état: Armed forces led by Kenan Evren announce they are dissolving the government
Cleveland Browns' Brian Sipe sets a club record with 57 pass attempts
Jerry Lewis's 16th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $31,500,000
Drury Gallagher wins the Manhattan Island Marathon Swim in an unofficial world record time of 6:41:35
Dwight Gooden's 11 strikeouts give him the NL rookie record of 236
Mary Decker Tabb Slaney sets a US record in the women's 3000 m (8:25.83)
Dan Marinothrows his 100th career touchdown pass, becoming the fastest QB in NFL history to do so
NBC premieres the TV movie "Popeye Doyle" as a sequel to "The French Connection" films
The Cleveland Browns become the first team in NFL history to have a play reviewed by instant replay, Chicago 41, Browns 31
"I Want Her" single released by Keith Sweat (Billboard Song of the Year, 1988)
Jerry Lewis's 22nd Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $39,021,723
South Africa frees Dutch anthropologist and ANCer Klaas de Jong
The Netherlands sends two minesweepers to the Persian Gulf
Elektra Records releases "...And Justice for All," the fourth studio album by American heavy metal band Metallica
NY Daily News reports boxerMike Tysonis seeing a psychiatrist
Securities and Exchange Commission accuses Drexel of violating securities laws
Brigham Young University quarterback Ty Detmer passes NCAA record 11,606 yards
Die Laughing wins Messenger Stakes
First South African international gymnastics competition in 25 years

The New WKRP in Cincinnati, an ensemble TV sitcom sequel created by Hugh Wilson and set in a struggling radio station, debuts in syndication; three members of the original series cast reprise their roles
Army of Ciskei homeland kills 28 ANC demonstrators
Baseball Commissioner Faye Vincent resigns
Former owner and team president of the Milwaukee Brewers, Bud Selig, is appointed interim Commissioner of Major League Baseball and holds office until becoming official Commissioner in 1998
Brazil votes on importing monarchy
Late-night talk show "The Chevy Chase Show" debuts on FOX TV, but it is canceled after six weeks
St. Louis Cardinals outfielder Mark Whiten hits 4 home runs and 12 RBIs in a 15-2 win against the Cincinnati Reds
Jingyi Le, Ying Shan, Ying Le, and Bin Lu swim the 4x100 freestyle in 3:37.91
Space Shuttle Endeavour (STS-69) launches into orbit
First test flight of the USAF F-22 Raptor takes place
Magnitude 5.9 earthquake rocks Athens and ruptures a previously unknown fault, killing 143, injuring over 500, and leaving 50,000 people homeless
The US Federal Trade Commission approves Chevron's bid to buy Texaco
Hurricane Ivan, a Category 5 hurricane, hits Grenada, killing 39 people and damaging 90% of its buildings
First presidential election is held in the Republic of Egypt
US Government takes control of the two largest mortgage financing companies in the US, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac
Plane crash in Russia kills 43 people, including nearly the entire roster of the Lokomotiv Yaroslavl Kontinental Hockey League team
64 people are killed and 715 injured after a series of earthquakes in southwest China
Canada closes its Iranian embassy and expels Iranian diplomatic staff from Canada
US drone attack kills eight people in Kismayo, southern Somalia
Fifteen people are killed in a restaurant car bombing by Al-Shabaab in Mogadishu, Somalia
Fourteen Syrian rebels and two civilians are killed by heavy government shelling in Damascus
Tony Abbott becomes Prime Minister of Australia after the Liberal-National Coalition wins the election
Asteroid 2014 RC makes a close approach to Earth (39,900 km; 24,800 mi)
British archaeologists announce the discovery of a Neolithic "superhenge" under three feet of earth at Durrington Walls, a large ritual site near Stonehenge
15th Summer Paralympics open in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
An 8.2 magnitude earthquake hits southwest of Pijijiapan, Mexico, killing at least 90 people; it is the strongest Mexican quake in a century
Consumer credit reporting agency Equifax reports an earlier cyberattack could affect 143 million Americans
Rashid Khantakes the first hat-trick in the history of the Caribbean Premier League
Indian Space Agency loses contact with its Chandrayaan-2 moon lander just 2 km from landing on the Moon's surface
India overtakes Brazil to record the second-highest number of COVID-19 cases with 4.2 million

Tour Championship, Men's Golf, East Lake GC: Dustin Johnson wins by three strokes over Xander Schauffele and Justin Thomas; claims FedEx Cup, equaling the biggest winner's prize in golf, US$15 million
Wildfires burn a record 2 million acres in California during the 2020 fire season, more than the state of Delaware, according to Cal Fire
El Salvador becomes the first country to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender
Mexican Supreme Court rules that making abortion a crime is unconstitutional, setting an important precedent
Taliban announce their interim government in Afghanistan with Mullah Mohammad Hassan Akhund in the most senior role
Texas Governor Greg Abbott signs a law restricting voting rights in the state, including limiting the use of drop boxes and empowering partisan observers
US records more than 40 million cases of COVID-19, daily cases at 161,000 (5 September), daily deaths at 1,560, with hospitalizations averaging 102,000 per day (New York Times figures)
Discovery of the earliest evidence of surgery from a 31,000-year-old skeleton with an amputated lower leg in a cave in East Kalimantan, Borneo, published in "Nature"
American "That '70s Show" actor Danny Masterson sentenced to 30 years to life in prison after a jury convicted him in May of two rapes in 2003
Intense flooding caused by the heaviest rainfall in 140 years in Hong Kong, Shenzhen, and southern Chinese cities turns streets into rivers
Cyclone Yagi, Asia's most powerful storm of the year, makes landfall on Hainan, China, before striking Northern Vietnam with winds of 230 km per hour (140 mph), killing at least 127 people
Roman army under GeneralTitusoccupies and plunders Jerusalem
Conference at Gloucester between English KingEdward the Confessorand his adversary, the Earl of Godwine; Godwine later goes into exile temporarily
Third Crusade:Richard Iof England defeatsSaladinin the Battle of Arsuf
William, Duke of Jülich-Cleves-Berg and ruler of the Duchy of Guelders, surrenders to EmperorCharles Vafter the emperor's forces subdue his territories
SultanSelim IIsucceeds to the Ottoman throne

English pirate Henry Every and his crew aboard the Fancy capture the armed Mughal trading ship Ganj-i-Sawai in the Arabian Sea in one of the most profitable raids in history, with loot valued between £325,000 and £600,000. The East India Company later compensates for the loss after Mughal EmperorAurangzebcloses five ports to English trade.
Battle of Borodino:Napoleon Bonapartewins a Pyrrhic victory against Russian General Mikhail Kutuzov in the most ferocious battle of the Napoleonic era; 70,000 are killed
Pedro I, son of King John VI, declares Brazil's independence from Portugal (Independence Day)
Frank Jamesand his gang are involved in a disastrous raid in Northfield, Minnesota, resulting in the death or capture of multiple gang members (Cole, Bob, and Jim Younger are arrested)

James Corbett knocks out 4 to 1 favorite and defending championJohn L. Sullivanin 21 rounds at the Olympic Club, New Orleans, for the world heavyweight boxing title; Sullivan's only defeat and his last fight
Boulder Dam (nowHooverDam) on the Colorado River between Nevada and Arizona begins operation
987 Dutch Jews are transported toAuschwitzConcentration Camp

Alberto Ascariof Italy, driving a Ferrari, wins the Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship with a victory in the season-ending Italian Grand Prix at Monza, securing the title by 12 points over his countryman Giuseppe Farina
"Journey to Italy," directed byRoberto Rosselliniand starringIngrid Bergmanand George Sanders, is released
US sweeps the medals in the men's discus at the Rome Olympics;Al Oerterwins his second of four career discus gold ahead of fellow Americans Rink Babka and Dick Cochran

Jimmy Page,Robert Plant,John Paul Jones, andJohn Bonhammake their live debut as Led Zeppelin (billed as The New Yardbirds) before about 1,200 students at Egegård School's Teen Club in Gladsaxe, Denmark
Scottish Matra-Ford driverJackie Stewartwins the Italian Grand Prix at Monza to secure his first Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship and his sixth Formula 1 win of the season
US courts findGeorge Harrisonguilty of "subconsciously" plagiarizing "He's So Fine" for his song "My Sweet Lord"
US PresidentJimmy Carterand Panama's General Omar Torrijos sign the Panama Canal treaties, guaranteeing Panama control of the Panama Canal after 1999
Desmond Tutubecomes Anglican Archbishop of Cape Town, South Africa
Failed assassination attempt on Chilean dictatorAugusto Pinochetkills five people
Guy Lafleur, Tony Esposito, and Brad Park are inducted into the NHL Hall of Fame
Bob Packwood, Republican Senator from Oregon, resigns instead of facing expulsion
RapperTupac Shakuris shot multiple times in a drive-by shooting in Las Vegas and dies 6 days later
30th Anniversary Celebration of singerMichael Jackson's solo career held at NYC's Madison Square Garden features an all-star lineup, including a reunion with his brothers for a medley of their hits
Chinese PresidentXi Jinpingannounces plans to develop a new "Silk Road Economic Belt" as part of the Belt and Road Initiative during a visit to Kazakhstan
Bruce Springsteenand the E Street Band perform their longest show in America at Citizens Bank Park in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, lasting 4 hours and 4 minutes
Eminembreaks a 36-year UK chart record for consecutive No. 1 albums, previously held jointly by ABBA and Led Zeppelin, with "Kamikaze"

Legacy Recordings releases "In the Blue Light," the fourteenth solo studio album by American singer-songwriterPaul Simon; the album consists of new recordings with altered arrangements of lesser-known songs from his catalog
RappersCardi BandNicki Minajcome to physical blows at a New York fashion event
US PresidentDonald Trumpsays he has canceled a secret meeting with the Taliban for peace talks at Camp David
New British Prime MinisterLiz Trussappoints her cabinet, and for the first time in British politics, none of the "great offices of state" are held by white men
Keir Starmermakes the first visit to Ireland by a British political leader in five years, meeting Taoiseach Simon Harris in Dublin
Edith Eleanor McLean is the first baby placed in an incubator at State Emigrant Hospital on Ward's Island, New York
Beginning of the Blitz as the German Luftwaffe bombs London for the first of 57 consecutive nights, losing 41 bombers as the Nazis prepare to invade Britain
Suzaku, 61st Emperor of Japan (930-46), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 952)
Thomas Erastus, Swiss physician and theologist, born in Baden, Switzerland (d. 1583)
Clemens Thieme, German composer, born in Grossdittmannsdorf, Electrorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1668)
Paul Esterhazy, Hungarian prince, composer and patron, born in Kismarton, Eisenstadt, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1713)
Tokugawa Ietsuna, 4th Japanese shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate (1651-80), born in Edo, Japan (d. 1680)
Ernest Augustus, Duke of York and Albany, German soldier and served under Emperor Leopold I, born in Osnabrück, Germany (d. 1728)
Maria Anna of Austria, Archduchess of Austria and Queen consort of Portugal, born in Linz, Austria (d. 1754)
Johan Ludvig Holstein, Danish politician (Minister of State, 1735-51), born in Lübz, Mecklenburg (d. 1763)
Jean Monnet, French theater impresario, born in Condrieu, Rhône, France (d. 1785)
Matthäus Günther, German painter, born in Peissenberg, Germany (d. 1788)
George-Louis Leclerc, count de Buffon, French naturalist and writer on natural history (Histoire naturelle, générale et particulière), born in Montbard, France (d. 1788)
François-André Danican Philidor, French composer and chess champion, born in Dreux, France (d. 1795)
Damasus Brosmann, Moravian composer, born in Fulnek, Margraviate of Moravia, Holy Roman Empire  (d. 1798)
Elisabetta de Gambarini, English composer, born in Holles Street, St Marylebone, Middlesex, England (d. 1765)
Thomas Coutts, Anglo-Scottish banker (Coutts & Co.), born in Ediburgh, Scotland (d. 1822)
Luigi Galvani, Italian anatomist and physicist ("animal electricity"), born in Bologna, Italy (d. 1798)
Joseph Legros, French singer and composer who was the most prominent haute-contres of his generation, born in Monampteuil, France (d. 1793)
Johan Tobias Sergel, Swedish sculptor, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1814)
Willem Bilderdijk, Dutch poet, historian, lawyer, and linguist (Luchtreis; My Relief), born in Amsterdam, Dutch Republic (now Netherlands) (d. 1831)
Heinrich David Stölzel, German horn player who developed the first valve for brass instruments, born in Schneeberg, Electrorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1844)
Bernardus Johannes Dibbets, Dutch baron and general-major (Maastricht), born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 1839)
František Max Kníže, Czech composer, born in Drahelčice Kingdom of Bohemia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1840)
Samuel Wilberforce, English bishop and one of the greatest public speakers of his day (remembered for his opposition to Darwin's theory of evolution), born in London (d. 1873)
Hermann Heinrich Gossen, Prussian economist, born in Düren, Germany (d. 1858)
William Hemsley Emory, American Major General (Union Army), born in Queen Anne's County, Maryland (d. 1887)
Howell Cobb, American politician (U.S. Secretary of Treasury), born in Jefferson County, Georgia (d. 1868)
John McDouall Stuart, Australian explorer, born in Dysart, Fife, Scotland (d. 1866)
Louise of Hesse-Kassel, Queen of Denmark, born in Kassel, Electorate of Hesse, Imperial Confederate of Germany (d. 1898)
Thomas Talbot, American politician, 31st Governor of Massachusetts, born in Cambridge, New York (d. 1885)
Adriaan van Bevervoorde, Dutch journalist (History of Holland), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1851)
Thomas A. Hendricks, 21st Vice President of the United States (D), born in Fultonham, Ohio (d. 1885)
August Kekule von Stradonitz, German chemist who discovered structure of benzene ring, born in Darmstadt, rand Duchy of Hesse by the Rhine, German Confederation (d. 1896)
Ferdinand Vandeveer Hayden, American geologist (Geograph Survey 1859-86), born in Westfield, Massachusetts (d. 1887)
Alexandre Falguière, French sculptor and painter, born in Toulouse, France (d. 1900)
August Toepler, German physicist, born in Brühl bei Bonn, Germany (d. 1912)
Olive Oatman, American (as a teen captured and released by Native Americans), born in La Harpe, Illinois (d. 1903)
Johannes Zukertort, German chess player, born in Lublin, Russian Empire (d. 1888)
Donald Petrie, Scottish botanist and educator noted for his work in New Zealand, born in Edinkillie, Scotland (d. 1925)
David King Udall, American politician and Mormon leader, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1938)
Edward Asahel Birge, American pioneer in limnology, born in Troy, New York (d. 1950)
William Friese-Greene, British photographer and inventor (motion pictures), born in Bristol, England (d. 1921)
Willem Hubert Nolens, Dutch Catholic priest, politician (MP, 1896-1931) and law professor (University of Amsterdam, 1909-26), born in Venlo, Netherlands (d. 1931)
Ernest Halliwell, South African cricket wicketkeeper (8 Tests), born in Ealing, England (d. 1919)
Giovanni Tebaldini, Italian composer, born in Brescia, Lombardy, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1952)
Tristan Bernard, French playwright and novelist, born in Besançon, Doubs, France (d. 1947)
Albert Bassermann, German actor (Foreign Correspondent, Madame Curie), born in Mannheim, Baden, German Confederation (d. 1952)
Camilo Pessanha, Portuguese poet (Clepsydra), and Chinese translator, born in Coimbra, Portugal (d. 1926)[1]
Evan Williams, American oratorio tenor, born in Mineral Ridge, Ohio (d. 1918)
J. P. Morgan, Jr. [John Pierpont Morgan, Jr], American financier and son of J.P. Morgan, Sr., (J.P. Morgan & Co.), built the Morgan Library, born in Irvington, New York (d. 1943)
Margo Scharten-Antink, Dutch author (Catherina, Sprotje), born in Zutphen, Netherlands (d. 1957)
Aleksandr Kuprin, Russian author (Pojedinok), born in Narovchat, Penza Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1938) [OS=Aug 26]
Thomas Curtis, American athlete (Olympic 1896 Gold), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1944)
C. J. Dennis, Australian poet (The Songs of a Sentimental Bloke), born in Auburn, South Australia (d. 1938)
Mike O'Neill, Irish-American baseball player, born in Maum, County Galway, Ireland (d. 1959)
Attilio Brugnoli, Italian pianist and composer, born in Rome, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1937)
Kurt von Wolfurt [Wolff], German composer, conductor, peadagogue, and musicologist, born in Lettin, Russian Empire (d. 1957)
Edith Sitwell, English poet and author (Wheels), born in Scarborough, North Yorkshire (d. 1964)
Albert Plesman, Dutch aviation pioneer and founder (KLM), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1953)
Bruce Frederick Cummings, English author (Journal of a Disappointed Man), born in barnstaple, England (d. 1919)
Roscoe Karns, American actor (Capt Shafer-Hennesey), born in San Bernardino, California (d. 1970)
Leslie Hore-Belisha, British politician (Minister of Transport, 1934-37; War Secretary, 1937–40), born in Hampstead, London (d. 1957)
Vic Richardson, Australian cricket batsman and captain (19 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 138; South Australia) and SANFL player (Magarey Medal 1920 Sturt FC), born in Adelaide, Australia (d. 1969)
Leendert Antonie Donker, Dutch Minister of Justice (PvdA), born in Almkerk, Netherlands (d. 1956)
Emerson Treacy, American actor (California Straight Ahead, Prowler), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1967)
Taylor Caldwell [Janet Miriam Holland Taylor Caldwell], Anglo-American novelist (Dynasty of Death, Dear and Glorious Physician), born in Manchester, England (d. 1985)
Roy Barcroft [Howard Ravenscroft], American actor (Oklahoma!; Freckles), born in Crab Orchard, Nebraska (d. 1969)
John Whitley, British air-marshal during WWII, born in Devonport, England (d. 1997)
Philip Herschkowitz, Romanian composer, born in Iași, Romania (d. 1989)
Ahmet Adnan Saygun, Turkish composer, born in İzmir, Ottoman Empire (d. 1991)
Max Kaminsky, American jazz trumpeter and bandleader (Max Kaminsky Orchestra), born in Brockton, Massachusetts (d. 1994)
Michael DeBakey, Lebanese-American cardiac surgeon and artificial heart pioneer, born in Lake Charles, Louisiana (d. 2008)
Jo Juda, Dutch musician (1st concertmaster of the Amsterdam Concertgebouw Orchestra), born in Amsterdam (d. 1985)
Natalia Dmitrevna Shpiller, Czech-Russian lyric soprano, born in Kyiv, Russian Empire (d. 1995)
John Shea, American 500m/1500m speed skater (Olympic gold 1932), born in Lake Placid, New York (d. 2002)
Todor Zhivkov, Bulgaria communist leader of the People's Republic of Bulgaria (PRB), 1954-89, born in Pravets, Kingdom of Bulgaria (d. 1998)
Anthony Quayle, British actor (Anne of 1000 Days, Lawrence of Arabia), born in Ainsdale, Southport, Lancashire (d. 1989)
Oswald Szemerényi, Hungarian linguist, born in London (d. 1996)
Wim van der Grinten, Dutch lawyer and KVP politician (State Secretary of Economic Affairs), born in Nijmegen, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Billy Hughes, British educationist and Labour Party politician, born in Swindon, England (d. 1995)
Graeme Bell, Australian jazz pianist, band leader, and composer, born in Richmond, Victoria, Australia (d. 2012)
Hugo Pfister, Swiss composer, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1969)
Ian Ball, Indian newspaper photographer, born in Dhariwal, India (d. 1995)
James Van Allen, American physicist (discovered Van Allen radiation belts), born in Mount Pleasant, Iowa (d. 2006)
Jacob Lawrence, African-American artist (Sanitarium), born in Atlantic City, New Jersey (d. 2000)
John Cornforth, Australian-British chemist (Nobel Prize 1975), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2013)
Carolyn Cudone, American golfer (US Senior Women's Amateur 1968-72; most consecutive wins in any USGA championship), born in Staten Island, New York (d. 2009)
Robin Lorimer, Scottish publisher (New Testament from Greek into Scots), born in Glasgow (d. 1996)
Alberic Schotte, Belgian cyclist (World C'ship gold road race 1948, 50), born in Kanegem, Belgium (d. 2004)
Al Caiola, American guitarist (Bonzanza theme), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 2016)
Harri Webb, Welsh poet and nationalist, born in Swansea, Wales (d. 1994)
Arthur Ferrante, American pianist and composer (Ferrante & Teicher - Exodus), born in New York City (d. 2009)
Josep Lluís Núñez, Spanish President of FC Barcelona (1978 - 2000), born in Guriezo, Cantabria, Spain (d. 2018)
Kirill Molchanov, Russian-Soviet composer, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1982)
Lucien Jarraud, Canadian radio host, born in Paris (d. 2007)
Madeleine Dring, British composer and pianist, born in Harringay, England (d. 1977)
Peter Lawford, British actor, producer and socialite (Mrs Miniver, Thin Man), born in London, England (d. 1984)
Bridie Gallagher, Irish singer (A Mother's Love's A Blessing, The Boys from County Armagh), born in Donegal, Ireland (d. 2012)
Charles Braswell, American actor (Only Game in Town), born in McKinney, Texas (d. 1974)
Daniel K. Inouye, American lawyer, politician (US Senator from Hawaii, 1963-2012; US Representative, 1959-63), and decorated WWII army veteran, born in Honolulu, Territory of Hawaii (d. 2012)[1]
Hugh Aitken, American composer, born in New York City (d. 2012)
Leonard Rosenman, American Academy and Emmy Award-winning film and television composer (Rebel Without A Cause; Star Trek IV; Bound for Glory; Sybill), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2008)
Allan Blakeney, Canadian politician (10th Premier of Saskatchewan), born in Bridgewater, Nova Scotia (d. 2011)

Laura Ashley, Welsh fashion designer, businesswoman and co-founder of Laura Ashley, born in Dowlais, Merthyr Tydfil,
Wales (d. 1985)
Don Messick, American cartoon voice actor (The Flintstones - "Bamm-Bamm"; The Jetsons - "Astro"; Scooby-Doo), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1997)
Donald J. Irwin, American politician (32nd Mayor of Norwalk, Connecticut), born in Rosario, Argentina (d. 2013)
